summary: DHH comments on the launch of DAGEN, the first new national Danish newspaper since World War II, scheduled for October 2002. He shares skepticism about its business model, particularly the decision to limit advertising to a single full-page ad per issue. Citing a fellow blogger's prediction, he draws a parallel to a Copenhagen indie movie theater that similarly tried to avoid commercials but eventually caved. He questions whether the target demographic of high earners will switch from screens back to print.
key_points: DAGEN would be Denmark's first new national newspaper since WWII, targeting high-income readers
The single-ad-per-issue business model is predicted to fail, drawing comparison to a Copenhagen theater that abandoned its no-commercials policy
The newspaper faces the challenge of luring affluent readers away from screens back to print media
At roughly 4,138 kr. for 300 issues, the premium pricing reflects its upscale positioning
money_quotes: DAGEN will be the first new national Danish newspaper to leave the presses since WW2
Read my lips: It. Wont. Work.
Whether they'll care enough to break with their current brand or switch back from the screen to dead trees will be interesting to watch
key_insight: Idealistic media business models that reject advertising revenue tend to collapse under economic reality, especially when competing against the convenience of digital screens.
